1
.  Remote sensing systems have been receiving considerable
4 attention due to technological advancements making sensors
0 more affordable and applicable [1]. One of the main problems
5
with remote sensing systems is keeping the information fresh
2
: at  the remote monitors. Recently,  several  freshness metrics
v have been proposed to quantify information freshness. The
i
prior of  these metrics is the age of  information (AoI) [2],
X
r [3] that quantifies information freshness by keeping track of
a
how long ago the latest received information packet had been
generated. However, it is argued in [4] that AoI may fall short
of capturing freshness in certain estimation problems since it
does not consider the dynamics of the sampled process. Partic-
ularly, even though the latest received packet may have been
generated a long time ago, it is possible that the source may not
have changed since then, and therefore, the packet can still be
fresh. Similarly, a recently received packet may contain stale
information if the source has already changed its state after
the packet was generated. Stemming from these drawbacks
inherent to AoI, [4] proposes an alternative freshness metric,
namely, age of incorrect information (AoII) that penalizes the
mismatch between the source and its estimation over time, and
regardless of when it is sampled, it defines the estimation as

of Age of Incorrect
emote Estimation
1  2
Ulukus , and Nail Akar
College Park, MD, USA
, Ankara, Tu¨rkiye
Fig. 1.  A remote estimation system with the source process Xt and the
monitor process Xˆt. At each time slot,  the source can sample the process
Xt, and initiate a transmission. If the process changes before the end of the
time slot,  the transmission is preempted, otherwise,  it reaches the monitor
with probability σ,  i.e., packet  transmissions are geometrically distributed
with parameter σ. The monitor updates its estimation with received updates
(marked with dashed circles).
fresh if it is the same as the source. Another prominent feature
of AoII in contrast to AoI is that the monitor is not required
to get a new sample to bring the age down to zero since the
mismatch condition between the source and the monitor may
as well be brought to an end upon a transition of the source,
to the estimated value at the monitor.
Let us consider the remote estimation system in Fig. 1. For
the source process X and its remote estimation Xˆ  at time t,
t  t
the AoII process, denoted by AoIIt, is given by,
′  ′  ˆ
AoIIt = t − sup{t  : t ≤ t, Xt′ = Xt′},  (1)
which  is  in  line with  the original  formulation  in [4]  for
AoII, which considers a linear  time penalty function with
unit proportionality constant. However, in this paper, we focus
on minimizing arbitrary functions of AoIIt  and estimation
value j which is denoted by fj(AoIIt), named as the AoII
penalty functions. This dependence on the estimation value is
motivated with the classical missile detection example in [5]
where incorrect estimation of the missile results in higher AoII
cost than the incorrect estimation of the no missile scenario.
The AoII metric is generally investigated for special DTMC
sources [4],  [6],  [7], and they obtain optimum transmission
policies  in order  to minimize  the average AoII with and
without sampling rate constraints, in the form of a threshold
policy,  and the optimization problem is cast as a Markov
decision process (MDP). On the other hand, in [8], a general
continuous-time Markov chain (CTMC) source is studied for
the first  time,  to the best of our knowledge.  In [9],  it  is
shown that using a single threshold push-based AoII policy
is suboptimal  for  the minimization of average AoII  in the
continuous-time setting,  and it  is proven that  the optimal

We consider a time-slotted remote estimation system with
an N-state DTMC source process Xt ∈ N that is irreducible
with the transition probability from state i to state j denoted
by qij . With the generate-at-will (GAW) principle, the source
can initiate a transmission of its observation and transmit it to
a remote monitor at the beginning of a time slot. We assume
that the state of Xt changes just before the end of the time slot,
and if it changes when there is an ongoing transmission, the
source preempts it to avoid sending incorrect information. If
the packet is not preempted, the transmission is successful with

Fig. 2.  A sample path of processes Xt, Xˆt,  and AoIIt  for a general
√  2
policy,  and AoII  penalty  functions f1(x) =  x,  f2(x) = x ,  and
f3(x) = x, where x = AoIIt. Successful  transmissions are shown with
blue arrows, and crossed red and purple arrows indicate failed and preempted
transmissions, respectively. AoII is reset upon synchronization (indicated with
star). Otherwise, it increases by one at every time slot as long as the mismatch
condition between Xt and Xˆt stays.
probability σ at the end of the time slot, or the transmission
continues with probability 1−σ. This system model is depicted
in Fig. 1.
The remote monitor estimates the source process using the
ˆ  ′  ′
latest  received information,  i.e., Xt = Xt  where t  is the
generation time of  the  latest  successful  transmission. This
estimation rule is widely used in the literature except some
recent works [17], [18] which consider a maximum a posteriori
(MAP) estimator for which the monitor updates its estimation
with the most likely state without receiving an update.
The mismatch between X and Xˆ  is measured with the
t  t
AoII process defined in (1), where fj(x) corresponds to the
AoII penalty function for estimation j. In Fig. 2, an example
√
scenario for N = 3 and penalty functions f1(x) =  x,
2
f2(x) = x ,  and f3(x) = x, where x = AoIIt.  In this
example,  the transmission initiated at  time slot 4 fails, and
the transmission at time slot 9 is preempted because of state
change at  time slot 10. On the other hand,  transmissions
initiated at time slots 10 and 14 succeed and result in a change
in the estimation process Xˆ . Notice that, at time slot 6, AoII
t
is reset without a successful  transmission since the original
process Xt transitions to the estimated value.
We assume instantaneous feedback from the monitor to the
source. Therefore, the source is always aware of the estimation
and AoII processes. The objective of this work is to find a
transmission policy for the source that minimizes the average
cost,
L
1 (cid:88)
min  lim  costℓ  (2)
δℓ  L→∞ L
ℓ∈{1,...,L}  ℓ=1
where
costℓ = fXˆ (AoIIℓ) + λδℓ,  (3)
ℓ
δℓ  is one if a transmission is initiated at  time ℓ, and zero
otherwise, and λ denotes a relative weight assigned for the
transmission cost. It is worth noting that,  this unconstrained
optimization problem can be iteratively used to solve a con-
strained optimization problem where the constraint is given as
an upper bound on the average transmission rate, by applying
the Lagrangian method, see for example [9].

In this section, we consider an estimation-based transmis-
sion policy for which the sensor  initiates a transmission if
incorrect  information lasts longer  than a threshold value τj
when the estimation is Xˆ(t) = j. Then, we obtain a one-
dimensional  embedded DTMC from the  three-dimensional
joint process (X , Xˆ , AoII ), and formulate the minimization
t  t  t
problem in (2) as an SMDP. In order to obtain the embedded
DTMC, we first need to define embedded points (EP).
Definition 1 (Embedded Points) A time point t0 is called an
embedded point (EP) with embedded value (EV) Ej  if X(t)
and Xˆ(t) are just  synchronized at  t = t ,  i.e., Xˆ(t ) =
0  0
X(t ) = j, Xˆ(t − 1) ̸= X(t − 1).
0  0  0
The interval between the EP t0 with EV Ej and the next
EP is called a cycle of type j, or cycle-j in short. A cycle-
j  is divided into two separate intervals with the first one
called the in-sync interval during which the source process
and estimation process have the same value, i.e., X(t) = Xˆ(t)
with its duration denoted by Hj . The second interval starts
when the synchronism between the source and the estimation
processes is broken and lasts until the beginning of the next
cycle. This interval  is called the out-of-sync interval whose
duration is denoted by Tj . During the out-of-sync interval,
the source initiates a transmission whenever the AoII process
exceeds the threshold τj which needs to be obtained for each
j to minimize the objective function in (2). Additionally, we
denote the cycle-j duration by Dj = Hj + Tj ,  the total
(cid:80)Tj
AoII cost by Aj =  t=1 fj(t),  and the total number of
transmission attempts in cycle-j by Cj .
A sample path with two complete cycles is illustrated in
Fig. 3. A green arrow denotes each transmission attempt. We
are initially at EV E3 and we transition to EV E2 after the
second transmission is completed when X(t) = 2. On the
other hand,  the second cycle ends with a transition of  the
original process X(t) from state 3 to the estimation value
2. For this example, the realizations of the random variables
defined in the previous paragraph are the following. Duration
(1)  (2)
of cycles are D3  = 5, D2  = 5, number of transmission
(1)  (2)  (1)
attempts C3  = 2, C2  = 2, and total AoII cost A3  =
(2)
1 + 2 + 3 = 6, and A2  = 1 + 4 = 5, where the subscript
indicates the type of the cycle and superscript corresponds to
the cycle index.  In addition,  threshold values are chosen as
τ3 = 1, and τ2 = 2,  thus the source initiates transmission
at each time slot when the AoII process is greater or equal
than the corresponding threshold. Subsequently, we construct
a DTMC whose states are the EVs Ei, i ∈ N, and construct
an SMDP as follows.
• Embedded values are the states of the problem with the
state space E = {E1, . . . , EN}.
• For each state Ej , the action is the value of the threshold
τj ∈ N, where N denotes the set of non-negative integers
which then constitutes the action space of the problem.
• There are two costs of this problem, they are respectively
age penalty cost, and transmission cost. For state Ej and

Fig. 3. A sample path with two complete cycles for a threshold policy.
action τj , we denote them with aj(τj) = E[Aj], and
cj(τj) = E[Cj], respectively, and the expected total cost
of the problem is denoted by rj(τj) = aj(τj) +λcj(τj).
• Similarly, the expected duration for state Ej for the same
action equals dj(τj) = E[Dj].
• Lastly, pji(τj) denotes  the  transition probability from
state Ej to the next state Ei when the action τj is applied.
The optimal deterministic policy that minimizes the opti-
mization problem in (2) can be obtained by policy-iteration
algorithm from Algorithm 1 [10], and we use parameters dj ,
rj , and Pji by omitting τj to denote their values for optimum
policy. In each iteration, threshold values that minimize (10)
are obtained with any line-search algorithm, and for practical
purposes, the action space N can be truncated by τmax.
By using the SMDP parameters,  the time average of  the
cost for the infinite horizon can be expressed as,

IV. DUAL-REGIME ABSORBING MARKOV CHAINS AND
PHASE-TYPE DISTRIBUTIONS
The following is needed to follow the proposed analytical
method for absorbing Markov chains (AMC) which refers to
discrete-time Markov chains with absorbing states. Consider
an AMC Y (t) with K transient and L absorbing states.

Let the
A B
transition matrix of this DTMC be written as [ 0 0 ], where
AK×K and BK×L are  the  transient probability transition
sub-matrix (TPTS) and absorbing probability transition sub-
matrix (APTS) corresponding to the transition probabilities
among the transient states, and from the transient states to
the absorbing states, respectively. In this case, we say Y (t)
is  an AMC characterized with  the  triple  (A,B, β),  i.e.,
Y (t) ∼ AMC(A,B, β), where β = {βi} is the 1 ×K initial
probability vector (IPV) with βi denoting the initial probability.

metadata.section_title: Dual-regime Absorbing Markov Chains and Phase-type Distributions

text:
The following is needed to follow the proposed analytical
method for absorbing Markov chains (AMC) which refers to
discrete-time Markov chains with absorbing states. Consider
an AMC Y (t) with K transient and L absorbing states. Let the
A B
transition matrix of this DTMC be written as [ 0 0 ], where
AK×K and BK×L are  the  transient probability transition
sub-matrix (TPTS) and absorbing probability transition sub-
matrix (APTS) corresponding to the transition probabilities
among the transient states, and from the transient states to
the absorbing states, respectively. In this case, we say Y (t)
is  an AMC characterized with  the  triple  (A,B, β),  i.e.,
Y (t) ∼ AMC(A,B, β), where β = {βi} is the 1 ×K initial
probability vector (IPV) with βi denoting the initial probability

In this section, we utilize the DR-AMC and DR-PH frame-
works which would allow us  to calculate  the SMDP pa-
rameters. More specifically, we model the DTMC transitions
from an EV Ej  to the next EV Ei with these frameworks,
and we then calculate variables dj(τj), aj(τj), and pji(τj)
for a given action τj . Each cycle-j starts with an EV Ej
which indicates X = Xˆ , and it  stays  in synchronization
t  t
for Hj . During synchronization, no transmission is initiated,
and no penalty occurs,  thus the total cost  is zero. The syn-
chronization is broken with the state change of Xt  to any
state i ̸= j, and it  lasts until  it  reaches an EP. We define
a process Yj(t) ∼ DR-AMC(βj1,Aj1,Aj2,Bj1,Bj2, τj) to
represent the out-of-sync interval of cycle-j. By Definition 3,
the out-of-sync interval duration Tj has a DR-PH distribution,
i.e., Tj ∼ DR-PH(βj1,Aj1,Aj2, τj). Transient states of the
process correspond to i ∈ N\j, and EVs Ei, i ∈ N are the
absorption states. The process Yj(t) has two regimes since
transition and absorption probabilities change when the regime
boundary τj is exceeded, and the source initiates transmissions
each time slot  in the second regime. The first regime starts
with the state transition of j → i of Xt with the probability
qji. Thus, we can define the initial probability of  transient
states for  this regime with a row vector {βj1}i = qji  for
i ̸= j. During this regime,  the source does not  initiate any
transmission,  thus only absorbing state Ej  can be reached
with a state change on Xt  to the estimated value j. This
regime lasts until an absorption to Ej occurs, or t reaches
the threshold τ . Table I provides the transition probabilities
from a transient state i from which the matrices Aj1 and Bj1
can be constructed. When the boundary is reached, the second
τj
regime starts with the initial probability vector βj2 = βj1Aj1,

In this section, we present numerical examples for validating
the SMDP model of the paper along with comparisons with

two benchmark policies:  i) single threshold (ST) policy for
which the source waits for a single system-wide threshold
τ while there is a mismatch between X and Xˆ , which is
t  t
a widely used mechanism in the literature [4],  [6], and the
value of τ which minimizes the overall cost is found by line
search, ii) random sampling (RS) policy [9] in which the source
initiates a transmission with α during the out-of-sync interval,
and again the value of α which minimizes the overall cost for
RS is found by line search. We also fix σ = 0.8 for all the
numerical examples.
In the first example, Xt has probability transition matrix Q1

analytically illustrated with a color map in Fig. 4. For each
integer λ between 0 and 75, the threshold pair that minimizes
(2)  is obtained with exhaustive search,  and shown with a
red circle.  In addition,  the optimum threshold pair obtained
by the proposed SMDP method is marked with a cross. The
perfect match between these marks verifies the optimality of
our algorithm.
In the second numerical example, we study two DTMC
information sources. The first 3-state source has probability

The second source with N = 10 states has a probability

For  this  source, we consider  the AoII  penalty  functions
1  2  1
fn(x) = nx + N+1−nx for state n. For a given weight
parameter λ, the proposed SMDP algorithm obtains the multi-
threshold optimum policy. The average cost obtained with
the SMDP algorithm along with the RS and ST policies
are depicted in Fig.  5. For ST and SMDP policies,  both
analytical and simulation results are obtained, and the strong
agreement between them verifies our analytical results. Notice
that since all AoII penalty functions are polynomials in the
examples, we employed the closed-form expression (17) for
finding the parameters of the SMDP model. On the other hand,
only simulation results are used for RS. We observe that our
proposed SMDP-based policy outperforms both benchmark
policies substantially, and all converge to the same policy when
λ → 0 which corresponds to the always transmit policy.
